t: 60–70% of childhood ADHD continues to some degree in adulthood (Faraone S.V. et al. meta-analysis, Psychol Med 2006).

Oppositional behavior disorder 40%, conduct disorder 14%, anxiety 25%, depression 15–30%, learning disorder 25–40%, tic disorders 7–20%, autism spectrum 30–50% (in RAS patients), substance use disorder risk increased 2–3 times in adolescence and adulthood.
74% (Faraone S.V., Larsson H. Mol Psychiatry 2019 meta-analysis of twin and family studies) — one of the highest heritability neurodevelopmental disorders.
Polygenic — hundreds of small-effect variants. GWAS (Demontis D. et al. Nat Genet 2019, n>55,000) identified 12 risk loci (FOXP2, SORCS3, DUSP6, and others).
DRD4, DRD5, DAT1 (SLC6A3) — candidate genes; effect size small.
CNV — rare; common variations are fundamental.
Dysfunction of the dopaminergic and noradrenergic systems
— particularly prefrontal cortex, striatum, cerebellum circuitry; stimulants reduce symptoms by acting on these systems.
A reduction in total brain volume and specific regions (caudate, putamen, prefrontal cortex) volume (Hoogman M. et al. Lancet Psychiatry 2017 ENIGMA-ADHD n>3000 — small effect sizes).
dysregulation between the default mode network and the executive control network.
(Barkley R.A.) — poor inhibition as core deficit; affects working memory, foreplanning, emotional regulation.
— Delayed reward sensitivity (Sonuga-Barke E.J. Neurosci Biobehav Rev 2003).
Preterm birth and low birth weight — increases risk 2–3 times.
Maternal use of tobacco, alcohol, and cannabis during pregnancy.
Maternal perinatal stress and severe psychosocial deprivation.
Sugar does not cause ADHD (Wolraich M.L. et al. JAMA 1995 meta-analysis).
Screen time / video games do not cause ADHD (association exists, but no evidence of causal direction — Ferguson C.J. Perspect Psychol Sci 2015).
Poor parenting does not create ADHD (heritability 74%); however, a highly conflictual family environment can affect symptom severity.
Failure to pay attention to details, careless mistakes.
Appears not to listen when spoken to directly.
Noncompliance with instructions, leaving tasks unfinished.
Persistent avoidance of tasks requiring sustained mental effort.
Movement of limbs, twisting in seated position.
Avoid sitting in places that require the patient to sit.
Running away or climbing in inappropriate places (in adults, feeling internal restlessness).
Behavior "as if driven by a motor".
Answer a question that has not been completed.
Do not interrupt others' conversations or activities.
hyperactivity dominant; attention short in play; behavioral control in social situations difficult.
Academic problems become prominent; inattention affects academic achievement.
motor hyperactivity decreases, inner restlessness and impulsivity remain; risk behavior increases.
Internal distress, difficulty with planning, impulsivity in relationships; functional impairment in work and family life.
6.1 Unified diagnostic criteria (DSM-5-TR · ICD-11 · NICE NG87 · AAP 2019 consensus points)
in children (≤16 years) at least 6 symptoms in each category
, must have lasted at least 6 months or be at a level not expected for age.
B. Several symptoms must have been present before age 12.
C. Symptoms in at least two settings
(e.g., home + school, home + work) should be observed.
— significant impact on social, academic, occupational activity.
— Does not occur in context of schizophrenia or other psychotic disorder; not fully explained by another psychiatric disorder.
presentation — inattentive / hyperactive-impulsive / combined; severity — mild, moderate, severe. Age of onset raised to 12 (in DSM-IV 7).
Parallel structure; 'co-morbid' qualifiers (autism, language disorder, intellectual disability).
diagnosis should be made by a multidisciplinary group (in children—pediatrician/psychiatrist; in adults—psychiatrist); structured interview and observation combined.
Diagnostic and treatment algorithm for ages 4–18 at the first medical assistance level
4–5 years — first-line parent training (PCIT, parent management training)
12–18 years — first line stimulant + behavioral intervention (with adolescent consent)
CADDRA Canadian ADHD Practice Guidelines (4th edition, 2018):
Parent and teacher (for children) or patient themselves (for adults) interview — symptom history, onset age, contexts.
Standardized rating scales—Conners-3 (parent/teacher/patient), Vanderbilt (AAP recommendation), SNAP-IV; for adults ASRS (Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale, WHO/Kessler).
Clinical interview — confirmation of symptoms in two contexts, documentation of functional impairment.
Medical and neurological examination — excluding somatic causes (thyroid, hearing, vision, anemia).
Intellectual and academic assessment (WISC-V, academic tests) — to differentiate learning disorder and intellectual disability.
Comorbidity screening — ODD, conduct disorder, anxiety, depression, tic, autism, substance use.
Sleep disturbance and obstructive sleep apnea screening (especially in hyperactive-impulsive type).
No functional impairment, or not observed in two contexts.
Serious behavioral disturbances, violation of others' rights.
Inattention due to anxiety; somatic complaints; onset generally late.
Affective symptoms are dominant; episodic course.
Affective episodes; no manic episode in childhood Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD).
Social-communication deficit, restricted-repetitive behaviors. Comorbidity high.
Academic domain-specific deficit; attention problems are context-dependent.
